summary: I was born in California in 1906, growing up in a working-class neighborhood with immigrant parents. My early life there was shaped by the challenges of discrimination and economic hardships. Despite these difficulties, my family’s support helped me navigate those times. California was the backdrop of my formative years and the place where my awareness of social justice began to take root.

The Great Depression was a pivotal time for me; it was during those years that I became deeply involved in activism. Witnessing widespread suffering around me made me want to take action, especially through labor organizing and civil rights work. The economic collapse of the 1930s exposed so many injustices, and it motivated me to fight for better working conditions and equal rights. That era truly shaped my commitment to social justice.
